title: WPS and WRF Installation and Working
text:
This blog provides complete instructions for installing, configuring, and running the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model along with the WRF Preprocessing System (WPS).
It is based on UCAR’s Build_WRF guide and adapted for modern systems (Ubuntu/WSL).


sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y build-essential gfortran gcc g++ m4 tcsh csh perl 
Check compilers:
# for version check
gcc --version
gfortran --version
m4 --version

# for location check



We perform this step to check if the Fortran compiler is built properly, if it is compatible with the C compiler, if the scripting languages csh, perl and sh are working properly.
For this, wel’ll make another directory for the test $HOME/TESTS.
cd $HOME/TESTS
wget http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/Fortran_C_tests.tar
tar -xvf Fortran_C_tests.tar


# Test1 Fortran Fixed Format
gfortran TEST_1_fortran_only_fixed.f
./a.out

# Test2 Fortran Free Format 
gfortran TEST_2_fortran_only_free.f90
./a.out

# Test3 C.
gcc TEST_3_c_only.c
./a.out

# Test4 Fortran calling a C function
gcc -c -m64 TEST_4_fortran+c_c.c
gfortran -c -m64 TEST_4_fortran+c_f.f90
gfortran -m64 TEST_4_fortran+c_f.o TEST_4_fortran+c_c.o
./a.out

# Test5 csh.
csh ./TEST_csh.csh

# Test6 perl
./TEST_perl.pl

# Test7 sh
./TEST_sh.sh




We’ll install libraries in $HOME/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES.
For installing tar files:
cd {path_to_dir}/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
wget http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/mpich-3.0.4.tar.gz http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/netcdf-4.1.3.tar.gz http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/jasper-1.900.1.tar.gz http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/libpng-1.2.50.tar.gz http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/zlib-1.2.7.tar.gz


cd $HOME/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
tar -zxvf netcdf−4.1.3.tar.gz
cd netcdf-4.1.3

# configure
./configure --prefix=$DIR/netcdf --disable-dap --disable-netcdf-4 --disable-shared

# compile and install
make -j2
make install



cd $HOME/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
tar -xvzf mpich-3.0.4.tar.gz
cd mpich-3.0.4

#configure
./configure --prefix=$DIR/mpich

# compile and install
make -j2
make install



cd $HOME/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
tar -zxvf zlib-1.2.7.tar.gz
cd zlib-1.2.7

# configure
./configure --prefix=$DIR/grib2

# compile and install
make -j2
make install



cd {path_to_dir}/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
tar -zxvf libpng-1.2.50.tar.gz
cd libpng-1.2.50

# configure
./configure --prefix=$DIR/grib2

#compile and install
make -j2
make install



cd {path_to_dir}/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES
tar -zxvf jasper-1.900.1.tar.gz
cd jasper-1.900.1

# configure
./configure --prefix=$DIR/grib2

# compile and install
make
make install




Add the following script at the end of ~/.bashrc using vi ~/.bashrc. Do not change the existing /.bashrc file.

```bash
export DIR=$HOME/Build_WRF/LIBRARIES

export CC=gcc
export CXX=g++
export FC=gfortran
export FCFLAGS=-m64
export F77=gfortran
export FFLAGS=-m64

export PATH=$DIR/netcdf/bin:$PATH
export NETCDF=$DIR/netcdf
export PATH=$DIR/mpich/bin:$PATH
export LDFLAGS=-L$DIR/grib2/lib
export CPPFLAGS=-I$DIR/grib2/include
export JASPERLIB=$DIR/grib2/lib
export JASPERINC=$DIR/grib2/include
Reload the file using source ~/.bashrc.



Perform these tests to ensure libraries are able to work with compilers.


cd $HOME/TESTS
wget http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/OnLineTutorial/compile_tutorial/tar_files/Fortran_C_NETCDF_MPI_tests.tar
tar -xvf Fortran_C_NETCDF_MPI_tests.tar



cp ${NETCDF}/include/netcdf.inc .
gfortran -c 01_fortran+c+netcdf_f.f
gcc -c 01_fortran+c+netcdf_c.c
gfortran 01_fortran+c+netcdf_f.o 01_fortran+c+netcdf_c.o -L${NETCDF}/lib -lnetcdff -lnetcdf
./a.out
Expected output:
C function called by Fortran
Values are xx = 2.00 and ii = 1
SUCCESS test 1 fortran + c + netcdf



cp ${NETCDF}/include/netcdf.inc .
mpif90 -c 02_fortran+c+netcdf+mpi_f.f
mpicc -c 02_fortran+c+netcdf+mpi_c.c
mpif90 02_fortran+c+netcdf+mpi_f.o 02_fortran+c+netcdf+mpi_c.o -L${NETCDF}/lib -lnetcdff -lnetcdf
mpirun ./a.out
Expected output:
C function called by Fortran
Values are xx = 2.00 and ii = 1
status = 2
SUCCESS test 2 fortran + c + netcdf + mpi




We’ll be building WRF inside $HOME/BUILD_WRF/ directory.
cd $HOME/Build_WRF
git clone https://github.com/wrf-model/WRF.git
cd WPS
While configuring the WPS, you will be provided with various options for compilers. I’m selecting option 3 which is Linux x86_64, gfortran (dmpar).
./configure
./compile >& compile.log &
tail -f compile.log
Executables in WPS/:
ls -ls *.exe
geogrid.exe
metgrid.exe
ungrib.exe


The WPS static geography data provides terrain, land use, soil type, albedo, etc., required by geogrid.exe. For this we’ll be using high resolution static data and storing it in $HOME/BUILD_WRF/geog_data directory.
cd $HOME/BUILD_WRF/geog_data
wget http://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/src/wps_files/geog_high_res_mandatory.tar.gz
tar -xvzf geog_high_res_mandatory.tar.gz
Verify the dataset
ls $HOME/Build_WRF/geog_data

# output - expected folders
albedo_ncep
greenfrac_fpar_modis
landuse_30s
...
soiltype_top
soiltype_bot
topo_gmted2010_30s




We’ll be building WRF inside $HOME/BUILD_WRF/ directory. While cloning, check if the WRF and WPS versions are same.
cd $HOME/Build_WRF
git clone https://github.com/wrf-model/WRF.git
cd WRF
git checkout v4.6.0
tar -xvzf v4.5.1.tar.gz
cd WRF-4.5.1
While configuring the WRF, you will be provided with various options for compilers. I’m selecting option 34 which is GNU (gfortran/gcc) (dmpar).
./configure
There will be a configure.wrf file after configuration. For compilation, I will be compiling em_real as I’ll be running WRF on real data.
./compile em_real >& log.compile &
tail -f compile.log
Executables in WRF/main/:
ls -las main/*.exe
ndown.exe 
real.exe 
tc.exe 
wrf.exe 




Edit namelist.wps



Defines domain size, resolution, projection, start/end dates, and geog data path.
Example: bash  &share  wrf_core = 'ARW',  max_dom = 2,  start_date = '2024-01-02_00:00:00','2024-01-02_00:00:00',  end_date   = '2024-01-03_00:00:00','2024-01-03_00:00:00',  interval_seconds = 10800,  io_form_geogrid = 2,  # defines output file format as NetCDF.  /  &geogrid  parent_id         =   1,   1,  parent_grid_ratio =   1,   3,  i_parent_start    =   1,  31,  j_parent_start    =   1,  31,  e_we              =  140, 121,  e_sn              =  130, 121,  dx = 27000,  dy = 27000,  map_proj = 'lambert',  ref_lat   =  22.00,  ref_lon   =  82.00,  truelat1  =  15.0,  truelat2  =  35.0,  stand_lon =  82.0,  geog_data_res = 'default','default',  geog_data_path = '$HOME/Build_WRF/geog_data'  /


Run Geogrid



Purpose: Generates static geographic fields (terrain height, land use, soil, etc.) for each domain. ```bash ./geogrid.exe
# Expected output files geo_em.d01.nc geo_em.d02.nc ```


Run Ungrib



Purpose: Converts GRIB-format meteorological data (e.g., GFS, ERA5) into intermediate WPS format.
Link Correct Vtable (refer “https://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/wrf/users/download/free_data.html” for correct Vtable list):
bash  ln -sf ungrib/Variable_Tables/Vtable.GFS Vtable
Link GRIB files:
bash  ./link_grib.csh <path_to_data>
Run:
```bash ./ungrib.exe
# Expected output files FILE:2024-01-02_00 FILE:2024-01-02_03 …
```


Run Metgrid



Purpose: Horizontally interpolates the meteorological fields from ungrib (GRIB → FILE:*) onto the model domain grid (geo_em). ```bash ./metgrid.exe
# Expected output files met_em.d01.2024-01-02_00:00:00.nc met_em.d01.2024-01-02_03:00:00.nc … met_em.d02.2024-01-02_00:00:00.nc ```




Once WPS preprocessing is complete (met_em files), the WRF model is run in two stages: real.exe (initialization) and wrf.exe (integration). 1. Prepare namelist.input
- This file controls model physics, runtime, and output frequency. - Key sections: - &time_control - defines start/end of simulation, run length, I/O settings. - &domains - defines domain size, nesting, vertical levels, grid spacing. Note: It must match namelist.wps values.

Run Real



Purpose: Converts WPS meteorological fields (met_em*) into initial and boundary conditions for WRF.
mpirun -np 2 ./real.exe

# Expected output files
wrfinput_d01
wrfinput_d02
wrfbdy_d01


Run WRF
mpirun -np 2 ./wrf.exe

# Expected output files
wrfout_d01_2024-01-02_00:00:00
wrfout_d01_2024-01-02_01:00:00
...
wrfout_d02_2024-01-02_00:00:00





libpng12.so.0 missing -> install libpng12-0 package

real.exe fails -> check date range in namelist.input vs met_em files

wrf.exe crashes early -> lower time_step (rule: 6 × dx in km)

wrf.exe still crashes after previous step -> reduce -np in mpirun
geogrid.exe fails -> verify geog_data_path and dataset presence

title: HTAP Emissions Analysis: India 2019 vs 2020
text:
This notebook presents a comparative analysis of emissions data from the HTAP dataset for the years 2019 and 2020.
The focus is on global patterns, with focus into the Indian subcontinent.
import xarray as xr
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Calculate and filter by percentiles

def calculate_percentiles(data_array, lower_per, upper_per):
    
    valid_data = data_array.values[~np.isnan(data_array.values)]
    
    if len(valid_data) == 0:
        return 0, 0
    
    lp = np.percentile(valid_data, lower_per)
    up = np.percentile(valid_data, upper_per)
    
    # print(f"{lower_per} percentile: {lp:.2e}")
    # print(f"{upper_per} percentile: {up:.2e}")
    
    return lp, up


def filter_data_by_percentiles(data_array, lp, up):

    filtered_data = data_array.copy()
    filtered_data = filtered_data.where((filtered_data >= lp) & (filtered_data <= up))
    
    return filtered_data



Prepare data for region

def prepare_data_for_region(data_array, region='world', shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):

    if region == 'world':
        print("Using global data extent")
        return data_array
    
    elif region == 'india':
        if region_bounds is None:
            min_lat, max_lat = 8, 38
            min_lon, max_lon = 65, 98
        else:
            min_lon, max_lon, min_lat, max_lat = region_bounds
        
        # Crop data to India bounds
        cropped_data = data_array.sel(lat=slice(min_lat, max_lat), lon=slice(min_lon, max_lon))

        # ds_2019 = ds_2019.sel(lat=slice(min_lat, max_lat), lon=slice(min_lon, max_lon))
        
        print(f"Data cropped to India: {min_lon}°E-{max_lon}°E, {min_lat}°N-{max_lat}°N")
        print(f"Shape: {data_array.shape} → {cropped_data.shape}")
        
        # Apply shapefile mask if provided
        if shapefile_path:
            try:
                gdf = gpd.read_file(shapefile_path)
                print(f"Shapefile loaded: {len(gdf)} features")
                
                # Create mask using shapefile
                lons, lats = np.meshgrid(cropped_data.lon.values, cropped_data.lat.values)
                points = [Point(lon, lat) for lon, lat in zip(lons.flatten(), lats.flatten())]
                
                mask = np.zeros(len(points), dtype=bool)
                boundary_geom = gdf.unary_union
                
                for i, point in enumerate(points):
                    mask[i] = boundary_geom.contains(point)
                
                mask = mask.reshape(cropped_data.shape[-2:])
                cropped_data = cropped_data.where(mask)
                
                valid_points = np.sum(mask)
                print(f"Masked: {valid_points}/{mask.size} points within India ({valid_points/mask.size*100:.1f}%)")
                
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Shapefile error: {e}. Using bounds only.")
        
        return cropped_data
    
    else:
        print(f"Unknown region: {region}. Using world.")
        return data_array



Set up map projections

def setup_map_projection(ax, region='world', shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):

    if region == 'world':
        ax.set_global()
        ax.add_feature(cfeature.COASTLINE, linewidth=0.5)
        ax.add_feature(cfeature.BORDERS, linewidth=0.3)
        # ax.add_feature(cfeature.OCEAN, color='lightblue', alpha=0.3)
        # ax.add_feature(cfeature.LAND, color='lightgray', alpha=0.3)
        
    elif region == 'india':
        # Set India extent
        if region_bounds is None:
            ax.set_extent([65, 98, 8, 38], crs=ccrs.PlateCarree())
        else:
            min_lon, max_lon, min_lat, max_lat = region_bounds
            ax.set_extent([min_lon, max_lon, min_lat, max_lat], crs=ccrs.PlateCarree())
        
        ax.add_feature(cfeature.COASTLINE, linewidth=0.8)
        # ax.add_feature(cfeature.BORDERS, linewidth=0.5)
        # ax.add_feature(cfeature.OCEAN, color='lightblue', alpha=0.3)
        # ax.add_feature(cfeature.LAND, color='lightgray', alpha=0.2)
        
        # Add shapefile boundaries if provided
        if shapefile_path:
            try:
                gdf = gpd.read_file(shapefile_path)
                gdf.plot(ax=ax, facecolor='none', edgecolor='black', 
                        linewidth=0.8, transform=ccrs.PlateCarree())
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Could not add shapefile overlay: {e}")
    
    # Add gridlines
    gl = ax.gridlines(draw_labels=True, alpha=0.3, linewidth=0.5)
    gl.top_labels = False
    gl.right_labels = False



Plot the data side by side

def plot_side_by_side(ds_2019, ds_2020, variable_name, month, lp, up, region='world',
                      shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):
   
    # Select data for the specific month
    data_2019 = ds_2019[variable_name].sel(time=ds_2019.time.dt.month == month)
    data_2020 = ds_2020[variable_name].sel(time=ds_2020.time.dt.month == month)
    
    # If multiple time steps for the same month, take the mean
    if len(data_2019.time) > 1:
        data_2019 = data_2019.mean(dim='time')
    else:
        data_2019 = data_2019.squeeze()
        
    if len(data_2020.time) > 1:
        data_2020 = data_2020.mean(dim='time')
    else:
        data_2020 = data_2020.squeeze()
    
    # Prepare data for region
    data_2019 = prepare_data_for_region(data_2019, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    data_2020 = prepare_data_for_region(data_2020, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Calculate percentiles for filtering
    print(f"Calculating percentiles for {variable_name} over {region}...")
    p10_2019, p90_2019 = calculate_percentiles(data_2019, lp, up)
    p10_2020, p90_2020 = calculate_percentiles(data_2020, lp, up)
    
    # Filter data by percentiles 
    filtered_2019 = filter_data_by_percentiles(data_2019, p10_2019, p90_2019)
    filtered_2020 = filter_data_by_percentiles(data_2020, p10_2020, p90_2020)
    # print(filtered_2019.shape, filtered_2019.min(), filtered_2019.max())
    # print(filtered_2020.shape, filtered_2020.min(), filtered_2020.max())
    
    # Create figure with side-by-side subplots
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 8),
                                   subplot_kw={'projection': ccrs.PlateCarree()})
    
    # Setup both map axes
    setup_map_projection(ax1, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    setup_map_projection(ax2, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Use the same colormap and scale for both plots
    vmin = min(filtered_2019.min().values, filtered_2020.min().values)
    vmax = max(filtered_2019.max().values, filtered_2020.max().values)
    
    # Plot 2019 data
    im1 = filtered_2019.plot(ax=ax1, transform=ccrs.PlateCarree(), 
                            cmap='viridis', add_colorbar=False, 
                            alpha=0.8, vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    region_title = region.capitalize()
    ax1.set_title(f'{region_title} 2019 {variable_name} - Month {month}', 
                  fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
    
    # Plot 2020 data
    im2 = filtered_2020.plot(ax=ax2, transform=ccrs.PlateCarree(), 
                            cmap='viridis', add_colorbar=False, 
                            alpha=0.8, vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    ax2.set_title(f'{region_title} 2020 {variable_name} - Month {month}', 
                  fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
    
    # Add colorbars
    cbar1 = plt.colorbar(im1, ax=ax1, shrink=0.6, pad=0.05)
    cbar1.set_label(f'{variable_name}', fontsize=10)
    
    cbar2 = plt.colorbar(im2, ax=ax2, shrink=0.6, pad=0.05)
    cbar2.set_label(f'{variable_name}', fontsize=10)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    # Add main title
    month_names = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun',
                   'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec']
    fig.suptitle(f'{region_title} {variable_name} Comparison: {month_names[month-1]} 2019 vs 2020', 
                 fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', y=0.95)
    
    plt.show()
    
    print(f"{region_title} data range - Min: {vmin:.2e}, Max: {vmax:.2e}")    
    return fig, filtered_2019, filtered_2020



Calculate difference

def calculate_difference(ds_2019, ds_2020, variable_name, month, lp, up, region='world',
                        shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):
    
    # Select data for the specific month
    data_2019 = ds_2019[variable_name].sel(time=ds_2019.time.dt.month == month)
    data_2020 = ds_2020[variable_name].sel(time=ds_2020.time.dt.month == month)
    
    # If multiple time steps for the same month, take the mean
    if len(data_2019.time) > 1:
        data_2019 = data_2019.mean(dim='time')
    else:
        data_2019 = data_2019.squeeze()
        
    if len(data_2020.time) > 1:
        data_2020 = data_2020.mean(dim='time')
    else:
        data_2020 = data_2020.squeeze()
    
    # Prepare data for region
    data_2019 = prepare_data_for_region(data_2019, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    data_2020 = prepare_data_for_region(data_2020, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Calculate difference (2020 - 2019)
    difference = data_2020 - data_2019
    
    # Calculate percentiles for filtering the difference
    print(f"Calculating percentiles for {region} difference data...")
    p10_diff, p90_diff = calculate_percentiles(difference, lp, up)
    
    # Filter difference by percentiles
    filtered_difference = filter_data_by_percentiles(difference, p10_diff, p90_diff)
    
    return difference, filtered_difference



Plot difference

def plot_difference(difference, filtered_difference, variable_name, month, region='world',
                   shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):
    
    # Create figure
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 10),
                          subplot_kw={'projection': ccrs.PlateCarree()})
    
    # Setup map
    setup_map_projection(ax, region, shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Plot difference data (centered at zero for diverging colormap)
    im = difference.plot(ax=ax, transform=ccrs.PlateCarree(), 
                                 cmap='RdBu_r', add_colorbar=False, 
                                 alpha=0.8, center=0)
    
    # Add colorbar
    cbar = plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.6, pad=0.05, extend='both')
    cbar.set_label(f'{variable_name} Difference (2020 - 2019)', fontsize=12)
    
    # Title
    month_names = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun',
                   'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec']
    region_title = region.capitalize()
    ax.set_title(f'{region_title} {variable_name} Difference (2020 - 2019): {month_names[month-1]}', 
                fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    
    # Print statistics
    # print(f"{region_title} difference statistics:")
    # print(f"  Mean difference: {difference.mean().values:.2e}")
    # print(f"  Min difference: {difference.min().values:.2e}")
    # print(f"  Max difference: {difference.max().values:.2e}")
    # print(f"  Standard deviation: {difference.std().values:.2e}")
    
    return fig



Complete flow

def complete_analysis(file_2019, file_2020, variable_name, month, lp, up, region='world',
                     shapefile_path=None, region_bounds=None):
    
    print(f"EMISSION ANALYSIS: {region.upper()} - {variable_name} - Month {month}")
    
    # Step 1: Read files
    print("\n1: Reading files")
    ds_2019, ds_2020 = read_files(file_2019, file_2020)
    
    # Check if variable exists
    # if variable_name not in ds_2019.data_vars:
    #     print(f"Error: Variable '{variable_name}' not found in datasets")
    #     print(f"Available variables: {list(ds_2019.data_vars)}")
    #     return None, None
    
    # Step 2: Plot side by side
    print(f"\n2: Plotting {region} {variable_name} side by side")
    fig1, filtered_2019, filtered_2020 = plot_side_by_side(ds_2019, ds_2020, 
                                                          variable_name, month, lp, up, region,
                                                          shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Step 3: Calculate difference
    print(f"\n3: Calculating {region} difference")
    difference, filtered_difference = calculate_difference(ds_2019, ds_2020, 
                                                          variable_name, month, lp ,up, region,
                                                          shapefile_path, region_bounds)
    
    # Step 4: Plot difference
    print(f"\n4: Plotting {region} difference")
    fig2 = plot_difference(difference, filtered_difference, 
                          variable_name, month, region, shapefile_path, 
                          region_bounds)
    
    print(f"\n{region.capitalize()} analysis complete!")
    return fig1, fig2, difference



Analysis functions

def analyze_world_emissions(file_2019, file_2020, variable_name, month):
    return complete_analysis(file_2019, file_2020, variable_name, month, 
                           region='world')

def analyze_india_emissions(file_2019, file_2020, variable_name, month, 
                           shapefile_path=None, india_bounds=None):
    return complete_analysis(file_2019, file_2020, variable_name, month, 
                           region='india', shapefile_path=shapefile_path,
                           region_bounds=india_bounds)

title: Experience
text:
Junior Research Fellow | Aug 2025 – Present
(PI: Prof. Nipun Batra)

Leading the development of a deep learning–based framework to learn spatio-temporal representations from WRF model outputs

Conducting multi-domain WRF simulations, GDAS preprocessing, and systematic spatio-temporal evaluation of model outputs

Designing and analyzing emulator architectures through spatial and temporal comparisons with WRF simulations

Producing reproducible analytics, visualizations, and documentation to support research outcomes

Acting as primary researcher on the project, mentoring junior students and coordinating experiments under PI supervision





Research Intern | July 2024 – April 2025

Developed and evaluated CNN-, YOLO-, and ConvLSTM-based models in PyTorch for satellite imagery analysis

Worked on tropical cyclone intensity estimation and temporal forecasting using multi-channel satellite and reanalysis data

Built robust preprocessing, normalization, augmentation, and class-balancing pipelines for multi-event, multi-year datasets

Achieved model accuracy up to 94% while ensuring robustness across diverse cyclone scenarios

Interpreted model outputs in collaboration with domain experts to ensure physical and meteorological consistency





Summer Intern | May 2024 – July 2024

Conducted literature review and dataset analysis for personality detection in sports-related multimedia data

Designed analytical frameworks and automated data collection and labeling workflows

Applied prompt engineering techniques to generate diverse, structured datasets for downstream evaluation

Gained hands-on experience deploying LLM-driven workflows in an industry research setting





Data Science Intern | Feb 2023 – May 2023

Designed automated data pipelines for cleaning, integrating, and analyzing large heterogeneous datasets

Developed and deployed Flask-based analytical web applications with backend databases

Delivered interpretable outputs and reports within short, iterative development cycles





ML Research Intern | June 2022 – Aug 2022

Curated and analyzed multilingual datasets across seven languages to support NLP model development

Performed comparative evaluation of deep learning and time-series models (DenseNet, ResNet, ARIMA)

Documented performance trade-offs and insights using structured visual and analytical summaries
